runs.intro_text:
Dot Estimation Task
In this task, you'll see 6 groups of dots on each screen.
Your job is to give your best guess for how many dots are in each group.
The dots will appear briefly and then disappear.
Please do not count the dots. We want your quick visual estimate and intuition.
You'll start with one quick practice round first.
Enter whole numbers only.
Begin
Powered by Qualtrics
seen_texts: Welcome. Before taking part in this study, please read the consent form below and click the continue (arrow) button at the bottom of the page if you understand the statements and freely consent to participate in the study. The survey is being conducted by a group of researchers at Erasmus University, and it has been approved by the Erasmus University Internal Review Board (ETH2526-0361). Participation in the study typically takes around 10 minutes and is strictly anonymous. All responses are treated as confidential, and in no case will responses from individual participants be identified. Rather, all data will be pooled and published in aggregate form only. Participation is voluntary. If you have further questions about this study or your rights, or if you wish to lodge a concern, please contact schley@rsm.nl. | Powered by Qualtrics

In this task, you will estimate how wealth is distributed across five equally large groups of people in the United States. Each group represents 20% of the population, ordered from the richest to the poorest. | Wealth means total assets minus debts. This includes things like savings, investments, retirement accounts, businesses, and property, minus loans and other debts. | Please give your best estimates. You do not need to look anything up. | Use the full range of values if needed. Your answers do not need to be rounded to multiples of 5 or 10. | Powered by Qualtrics

Below are five groups, ordered from richest to poorest. | For each group, estimate the total amount of wealth they hold. | Please enter your answer in trillions of dollars. For some context, as of April 2026, the 100 richest people in the world have a combined wealth of $7.2 trillion. | Give your best estimates. The only think you should note is that the top 20% should have more wealth than the second 20%, the second should have more than the middle, and so on. | Top 20% (richest) trillion dollars of total wealth | Second 20% (next richest) trillion dollars of total wealth | Middle 20% trillion dollars of total wealth | Fourth 20% (second poorest) trillion dollars of total wealth | Bottom 20% (poorest) trillion dollars of total wealth | Powered by Qualtrics
Powered by Qualtrics

experiment.title: Numerosity Task
instructionsText: You'll see a few groups of dots on the screen at the same time.
The dots will disappear before you answer, so use your intuition rather than trying to count.
experiment.endText: The task is complete. Your data have been prepared for export.

instructionsText: You'll see groups of dots on the screen.
Give your best estimate of how many dots there are.
Use your intuition and do not try to count.
Enter whole numbers only.
numerosity.readyText: Get ready for the next dot display
numerosity.promptSeparate: How many dots are on the screen?
numerosity.promptJoint: How many dots are in each group?

instructionsText: You'll see bars split into colored sections.
Give your best guess for the percentage in each section.
Decimals are fine. Just go with what looks right.
proportion.jointPrompt: What percentage of the whole bar is each section?
proportion.jointHelperText: Type your best guess for A through E, and try to make them add up to 100. Decimals are fine.
proportion.separatePrompt: What percentage of the bar is shaded?
proportion.separateHelperText: Type your best guess from 0 to 100. Decimals are fine.

In this task, you will estimate how wealth is distributed across five equally large groups of people in the United States. Each group represents 20% of the population, ordered from the richest to the poorest. | Wealth means total assets minus debts. This includes things like savings, investments, retirement accounts, businesses, and property, minus loans and other debts. | Please give your best estimates. You do not need to look anything up. | Use the full range of values if needed. Your answers do not need to be rounded to multiples of 5 or 10. | Powered by Qualtrics

Trillions of dollars of wealth per group | The population is divided into five equal groups (each 20%), ordered from richest to poorest. | Group: Top 20% (richest) | How much total wealth is owned by this group? | Please enter your answer in trillions of dollars. For some context, as of April 2026, the 100 richest people in the world have a combined wealth of $7.2 trillion. | Give your best estimates. The only think you should note is that the top 20% should have more wealth than the second 20%, the second should have more than the middle, and so on. | trillion dollars of total wealth | Powered by Qualtrics

runs.intro_text:
Proportion Judgment Task
In this task, you'll see one bar split into 5 colored sections.
Your job is to estimate what percentage of the whole bar each colored section takes up.
Your answers do not need to add up to 100.
Decimals are okay.
You'll start with one quick practice round first.
Begin
Powered by Qualtrics
seen_texts: Welcome. Before taking part in this study, please read the consent form below and click the continue (arrow) button at the bottom of the page if you understand the statements and freely consent to participate in the study. The survey is being conducted by a group of researchers at Erasmus University, and it has been approved by the Erasmus University Internal Review Board (ETH2526-0361). Participation in the study typically takes around 10 minutes and is strictly anonymous. All responses are treated as confidential, and in no case will responses from individual participants be identified. Rather, all data will be pooled and published in aggregate form only. Participation is voluntary. If you have further questions about this study or your rights, or if you wish to lodge a concern, please contact schley@rsm.nl. | Powered by Qualtrics

You do not need to look anything up. | Use the full range of values if needed. Your answers do not need to be rounded to multiples of 5 or 10. | Powered by Qualtrics

runs.intro_text:
Dot Estimation Task
In this task, you'll see 6 groups of dots on each screen.
Your job is to give your best guess for how many dots are in each group.
The dots will appear briefly and then disappear.
Please do not count the dots. We want your quick visual estimate and intuition.

Enter whole numbers only.

seen_texts: Welcome. Participation in the study typically takes around 10 minutes and is strictly anonymous. All responses are treated as confidential, and in no case will responses from individual participants be identified. Rather, all data will be pooled and published in aggregate form only. Participation is voluntary. If you have further questions about this study or your rights, or if you wish to lodge a concern, please contact schley@rsm.nl. | Powered by Qualtrics

blocks.name: Numerosity Practice

runs.intro_text:
Proportion Judgment Task
In this task, you'll see one bar split into 5 colored sections.
Your job is to estimate what percentage of the whole bar each colored section takes up.
Your answers do not need to add up to 100.
Decimals are okay.
You'll start with one quick practice round first.
Begin

experiment.title: Numerosity Task
instructionsText: You'll see a few groups of dots on the screen at the same time.
The dots will stay visible while you answer, so please use your intuition instead of counting.
experiment.endText: The task is complete. Your data have been prepared for export.

instructionsText: You'll see groups of dots on the screen.
Give your best estimate of how many dots there are.
Use your intuition and do not try to count.
Enter whole numbers only.
numerosity.readyText: Get ready for the next dot display
numerosity.promptSeparate: How many dots are on the screen?
numerosity.promptJoint: How many dots are in each group?

instructionsText: You'll see bars split into colored sections.
Give your best guess for the percentage in each section.
Decimals are fine. Just go with what looks right.
proportion.jointPrompt: What percentage of the whole bar is each section?
proportion.jointHelperText: Type your best guess for A through E, and try to make them add up to 100. Decimals are fine.
proportion.separatePrompt: What percentage of the bar is shaded?
proportion.separateHelperText: Type your best guess from 0 to 100. Decimals are fine.
